and data apps, offering 70+ chart types (scatter, line, bar, heatmaps, 3D, maps, financial), with built-in tooltips, zoom, pan, and export capabilities, making it ideal for exploring ML models, building dashboards, and sharing interactive reports.
graphs with Python. On a Plotly chart, it's possible to have
the chart as PNG, and more."
Zoom, pan, hover tooltips, export to PNG/SVG
Scatter, line, bar, heatmap, 3D, maps, financial, violin, etc.
MIT-licensed, stable for over a decade
Renders in Jupyter notebooks, standalone HTML, or Dash apps
Trusted by millions of data scientists and engineers
Why Plotly matters in ML Engineering
: Hover to see exact values, zoom to focus on regions
: Interactive loss curves, residual plots, feature importance
: Share interactive HTML reports with stakeholders
: Create production data apps with Dash (Python framework)
: Visualize embeddings, geospatial data, surface plots
"What sets Plotly apart is its capacity to produce not just static visualizations but also
. Its integration with web technologies allows for dynamic visualization that respond to user inputs to a more engaging and exploratory approach to data analysis."

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('data.csv')

# Scatter plot with single line
fig = px.scatter(df, x='feature1', y='feature2', color='category')
fig.show()

:
- Minimal code
- Automatic semantic mapping (color, size, hover)
- Beautiful defaults

import plotly.graph_objects as go

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(x=df['feature1'], y=df['feature2'], mode='markers'))
fig.update_layout(title='Plot Title', xaxis_title='X', yaxis_title='Y')
fig.show()

:
- Full control over every element
- Complex layouts, custom annotations

import plotly.express as px

fig = px.scatter(df, x='feature1', y='feature2', color='category', hover_data=['value'])
fig.show()

:
- Hover tooltips with exact values
- Color by category
- Zoom/pan

fig = px.line(df, x='time', y='value', color='category')
fig.show()

fig = px.bar(df, x='category', y='value', color='category')
fig.show()

import plotly.express as px

corr = df.corr()
fig = px.imshow(corr, text_auto=True)
fig.show()

fig = px.scatter_3d(df, x='x', y='y', z='z', color='category')
fig.show()

fig = px.scatter_geo(df, lon='longitude', lat='latitude', color='category')
fig.show()

fig = px.scatter(df, x='x', y='y', hover_data=['value1', 'value2'])
fig.show()
Hover shows exact values for each point.
Click and drag on any Plotly chart to zoom. Use toolbar to pan/zoom.

fig.write_image('plot.png')  # Export to PNG
fig.write_html('plot.html')  # Save as standalone HTML
is Plotly's Python framework for building

from dash import Dash, html, dcc
import plotly.express as px

app = Dash(__name__)

fig = px.scatter(df, x='x', y='y')

app.layout = html.Div([
    dcc.Graph(figure=fig)
])

app.run_server()

:
- No JavaScript required
- Build web apps with pure Python
- Production-ready dashboards
Best practices for Plotly in ML Engineering

fig = px.scatter(df, x='x', y='y', color='category')
fig.show()

fig = px.scatter(df, x='x', y='y', hover_data=['value'])
fig.show()

fig = px.scatter_3d(df, x='x', y='y', z='z')
fig.show()

Plotly charts rendered to HTML embed the full Plotly.js library (~3 MB) plus all data as JSON. A scatter plot with 100k points can produce a 50 MB HTML file, which is impractical for sharing. Use fig.write_html(include_plotlyjs='cdn') to reference CDN instead, reducing to ~10 KB.

Plotly renders in the browser via WebGL (for scatter plots) or SVG (for most others). SVG charts with >10k elements become slow to interact with. Downsample data or use WebGL-based traces (go.Scattergl) for large datasets.
Plotly is web-first. Exporting to PNG or PDF requires kaleido (pip install kaleido) which launches a headless browser and can be slow or fail in CI environments.

When using go.Figure() for fine-grained control, the API is significantly more verbose than Matplotlib for simple charts. For static plots without interactivity, Matplotlib or Seaborn produces cleaner code.
Plotly Express makes simple charts easy but switching to Graph Objects for advanced customisation requires understanding the full figure object hierarchy — the learning curve is steep.

Plotly Python is a wrapper around Plotly.js, a JavaScript visualisation library built on D3.js and WebGL. When you call fig.show() in a Jupyter notebook, Plotly serialises the figure as a JSON object containing traces (the data to display), layout (axes, titles, margins), and frames (for animations). This JSON is passed to the Plotly.js renderer which constructs the interactive SVG/WebGL chart in the browser.

Plotly Express is a high-level API that wraps go.Figure construction. A single px.scatter() call with color='column' internally creates a go.Scatter trace per category, sets the marker colour, and generates a legend. You can inspect and modify the underlying Graph Object: fig = px.scatter(...); fig.update_traces(marker_size=10). This two-layer architecture lets you start simple with Express and switch to Graph Objects for customisation without rewriting everything.

import plotly.express as px
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Show that px.scatter returns a go.Figure
df = pd.DataFrame({"x": np.random.randn(100), "y": np.random.randn(100), "group": np.random.choice(["A","B","C"], 100)})
fig = px.scatter(df, x="x", y="y", color="group")
print(type(fig))            # <class 'plotly.graph_objects.Figure'>
print(type(fig.data[0]))    # <class 'plotly.graph_objects.Scatter'>

# You can inspect and modify the underlying go objects
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=8, opacity=0.7))
fig.update_layout(
    title="Custom scatter",
    xaxis_title="Feature A",
    yaxis_title="Feature B",
    template="plotly_dark",
)
fig.show()

sharing reports with stakeholders who need to explore data interactively; building data apps or dashboards (Dash); visualising model outputs in Jupyter notebooks where hover tooltips add value; working with 3D data (embeddings, surfaces); creating geospatial visualisations.

generating plots for LaTeX papers (use Matplotlib); you need hundreds of plots in a loop for quick exploratory analysis (Seaborn is faster to code); the chart needs to run in a completely offline environment with no browser (Plotly HTML requires a browser to render).

The recommended pattern is to use Plotly Express for the initial chart, then update_traces() and update_layout() for customisation. This gives you the speed of Express defaults while retaining full control over every element.
as a pandas DataFrame. Plotly Express works directly with column names.

based on the relationship: px.scatter (two numeric variables), px.line (time series), px.bar (categorical comparison), px.histogram (distribution), px.imshow (matrix/heatmap), px.scatter_3d (3D data).
(color, size, hover_data) to encode additional dimensions without extra subplots.
to set titles, axis labels, colour schemes, and marker properties.
fig.show() for notebook display; fig.write_html('chart.html') for sharing; fig.write_image('chart.png') for static export.
Visualise training and validation loss across epochs with hover showing the exact values:

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Simulated training history
epochs = list(range(1, 51))
train_loss = [1.2 * np.exp(-0.06 * e) + 0.05 * np.random.rand() for e in epochs]
val_loss   = [1.2 * np.exp(-0.05 * e) + 0.08 * np.random.rand() + 0.02 for e in epochs]

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "epoch":      epochs * 2,
    "loss":       train_loss + val_loss,
    "split":      ["train"] * 50 + ["val"] * 50,
})

fig = px.line(
    df,
    x="epoch",
    y="loss",
    color="split",
    title="Training vs Validation Loss",
    labels={"loss": "Cross-Entropy Loss", "epoch": "Epoch"},
    color_discrete_map={"train": "#7dd3fc", "val": "#86efac"},
)
fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_dark",
    legend_title="Split",
    hovermode="x unified",
)
fig.write_html("training_curves.html")
fig.show()
Visualise model feature importances sorted descending, with colour encoding importance tier:

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer

# Train a model on breast cancer dataset
data = load_breast_cancer()
X, y = data.data, data.target
feature_names = data.feature_names

clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
clf.fit(X, y)

# Build importance DataFrame
importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "feature": feature_names,
    "importance": clf.feature_importances_,
}).sort_values("importance", ascending=True)

importance_df["tier"] = pd.qcut(
    importance_df["importance"], q=3, labels=["low", "medium", "high"]
)

fig = px.bar(
    importance_df,
    x="importance",
    y="feature",
    color="tier",
    orientation="h",
    title="Random Forest Feature Importances (Breast Cancer)",
    color_discrete_map={"low": "#64748b", "medium": "#fbbf24", "high": "#86efac"},
    labels={"importance": "Mean Decrease in Impurity"},
)
fig.update_layout(template="plotly_dark", height=700)
fig.write_html("feature_importance.html")
fig.show()

A team building a document clustering pipeline uses Plotly to visualise 2D UMAP projections of document embeddings, coloured by cluster label. Hovering over a point shows the document title and cluster name, making it easy for product stakeholders (not data scientists) to explore and validate the clusters before deploying them to production.

import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Simulated: replace with real UMAP output and cluster labels
np.random.seed(42)
n = 500
centers = {"Support": [0, 0], "Billing": [5, 3], "Technical": [-4, 4], "Feature Request": [3, -4]}

rows = []
for label, (cx, cy) in centers.items():
    x = np.random.randn(n // 4) + cx
    y = np.random.randn(n // 4) + cy
    titles = [f"{label} ticket #{i}" for i in range(n // 4)]
    rows += [{"x": xi, "y": yi, "cluster": label, "title": t} for xi, yi, t in zip(x, y, titles)]

df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

fig = px.scatter(
    df,
    x="x",
    y="y",
    color="cluster",
    hover_data=["title"],
    title="Support Ticket Clusters (UMAP 2D)",
    labels={"x": "UMAP-1", "y": "UMAP-2", "cluster": "Category"},
    opacity=0.7,
    color_discrete_sequence=px.colors.qualitative.Set2,
)
fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=5))
fig.update_layout(
    template="plotly_dark",
    legend_title="Cluster",
    width=900,
    height=600,
)
fig.write_html("ticket_clusters.html", include_plotlyjs="cdn")  # CDN ref keeps file small
fig.show()
print(f"Saved as ticket_clusters.html ({pd.Series([len(df)]).iloc[0]:,} points)")
Plotting all data points for large datasets.
Rendering 100k+ scatter points with SVG traces freezes the browser. Use go.Scattergl (WebGL) or downsample with df.sample(10_000) before plotting.
Not setting include_plotlyjs='cdn' for shared HTML.
The default write_html() embeds the full 3 MB Plotly.js bundle. For email attachments or shared links, use include_plotlyjs='cdn' to reference the CDN version.
fig.show() opens a browser window, which blocks execution in scripts. Use fig.write_html() or fig.write_image() for non-interactive contexts.

The default Plotly template has a white background that looks fine in notebooks but clashes with dark-mode dashboards. Set template='plotly_dark' for dark-mode apps or template='simple_white' for publications.
Mixing px and go traces on the same figure incorrectly.

Adding go.Scatter traces to a px figure via fig.add_trace() can conflict with Express's automatic axis and legend configuration. Always inspect the resulting layout with fig.layout to debug unexpected behaviour.

Plotly SVG traces handle up to ~10,000 points before browser performance degrades. Beyond this, switch to WebGL-based traces which move rendering to the GPU. For time-series with millions of data points, use plotly-resampler (pip install plotly-resampler) which dynamically downsamples the visible data as you zoom, maintaining interactivity while showing only ~1,000 representative points in the viewport at any zoom level.

import plotly.graph_objects as go
import numpy as np

# WebGL scatter for large datasets (100k+ points)
n = 100_000
fig = go.Figure(go.Scattergl(
    x=np.random.randn(n),
    y=np.random.randn(n),
    mode="markers",
    marker=dict(size=2, color="#7dd3fc", opacity=0.4),
))
fig.update_layout(title=f"WebGL scatter ({n:,} points — still fast)", template="plotly_dark")
fig.show()

# Dynamic resampling for time series
# pip install plotly-resampler
from plotly_resampler import FigureResampler
fig_rs = FigureResampler(go.Figure())
fig_rs.add_trace(go.Scatter(), hf_x=np.arange(1_000_000), hf_y=np.random.randn(1_000_000))
fig_rs.show_dash(mode="inline")  # only renders ~1000 visible points at a time

Plotly HTML exports embed all data as plain JSON within the HTML file. If the data contains PII, confidential business metrics, or proprietary signals, distributing the HTML file exposes all of it — even if the chart looks anonymised, the underlying JSON in the page source contains every data point. Before sharing HTML exports, audit the data included in the figure's fig.data attribute and strip or aggregate sensitive fields.

Dash applications that receive user input (filter dropdowns, date ranges) should validate all callback inputs server-side. Plotly's clientside callbacks run in the browser and cannot be trusted for data access control — authorisation must be enforced in the Python callback layer before querying data. For sensitive dashboards, add HTTP authentication (Dash Auth library, or a reverse proxy with OAuth) and serve over HTTPS.
with 70+ chart types, built-in tooltips, zoom, pan, and export.
: hover tooltips, zoom/pan, export to PNG/HTML.
is Plotly's Python framework for building production data apps and dashboards with no JavaScript.
